title: Schrödinger's Cat
definition: A thought experiment illustrating the paradox of quantum superposition applied to macroscopic objects. A cat in a box with a radioactive source and poison is simultaneously alive and dead until observed, highlighting the measurement problem in quantum mechanics.

proofs.description: The thought experiment demonstrates the absurdity of naively applying superposition to macroscopic objects
proofs.reference: Schrödinger, E. (1935). Die gegenwärtige Situation in der Quantenmechanik. Naturwissenschaften, 23(48), 807-812.

proofs.description: Experiments with larger quantum superpositions (buckyballs, small diamonds) push the boundary of the measurement problem
proofs.reference: Arndt, M., et al. (1999). Wave-particle duality of C60 molecules. Nature, 401, 680-682.

difficulty_levels.beginner: Schrödinger imagined a cat that is both alive and dead until you look. It shows how weird quantum mechanics seems when applied to everyday objects.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: The thought experiment illustrates the measurement problem: quantum systems exist in superposition until measured, but what constitutes a 'measurement'? When does the cat become definitively alive or dead?
difficulty_levels.expert: Schrödinger's cat was intended to mock the Copenhagen interpretation, not endorse it. Modern decoherence theory resolves the paradox: environmental interaction causes apparent collapse without requiring consciousness. The cat is never truly in superposition due to continuous decoherence from its environment.

extra_fields.historical_context: Proposed by Erwin Schrödinger in 1935 in response to the Copenhagen interpretation, specifically to highlight the absurdity of applying superposition to macroscopic objects.
extra_fields.limitations: Modern decoherence theory largely resolves the paradox; actual experiments never create macroscopic superpositions due to environmental decoherence.
extra_fields.creator: Erwin Schrödinger
